# Conclusion

Rigid pavements have remained on the periphery of machine-learning research on IRI prediction, not because
they are of lesser practical importance, but because no prior study has extended the explainable
ensemble-learning approach developed for asphalt pavements to JPCP, JRCP, and CRCP under a comparable, jointly
benchmarked framework. This study closes that gap using a reproducible pipeline built directly against the
FHWA LTPP Standard Data Release, spanning 1,552 flexible-pavement and 1,693 rigid-pavement records across the
U.S. and Canadian LTPP network.

An Optuna-tuned gradient-boosted ensemble, evaluated under a section-grouped train/test split that prevents
repeated visits to the same physical pavement from inflating reported accuracy, explains 41.4% of
out-of-sample IRI variance for flexible pavements and 22.0% for pooled rigid pavements using distress,
structure, and climate variables alone, rising to 69.8% and 39.1%, respectively, when each section's own
recent IRI history is included as an additional predictor. In both pavement families, the ensemble model
substantially outperforms a mechanistic-empirical-style linear regression and trains several times faster than
a comparably accurate random forest. SHAP-based interpretation recovers, without being given the functional
form of any mechanistic-empirical equation, the same distress mechanisms — transverse cracking and patching
for flexible pavements, joint faulting and load-transfer efficiency for rigid pavements — that decades of
pavement engineering research established by hand, while additionally identifying climate interactions that
fixed-coefficient equations cannot represent.

Three further results have direct implications for how LTPP data is used in pavement performance modeling.
First, evaluation protocol affects reported accuracy as much as model choice: a train/test split that does not
account for LTPP's repeated-visit panel structure can substantially overstate accuracy, and reporting
splitting strategy in sufficient detail for this to be assessed should be standard practice in LTPP-based
machine-learning studies. Second, traffic loading and load-transfer efficiency each contribute measurably but
modestly to model accuracy once properly imputed, indicating that neither should be excluded for missingness
nor treated as the dominant explanatory variable. Third, at the sample sizes currently available in LTPP, a
single pooled rigid-pavement model spanning JPCP, JRCP, and CRCP — distinguished by a pavement-type indicator
— generalizes better than separate models fit to each sub-family, despite their differing distress mechanisms.

These findings have practical relevance for both highway agencies and the pavement engineering research
community. Agencies prioritizing maintenance across mixed flexible and rigid networks can apply a common
modeling framework rather than maintaining separate tools built on incompatible assumptions, selecting between
the structural and operational model variants according to whether the underlying question is diagnostic
(identifying what is driving roughness) or predictive (determining when a section should next be inspected).
For the research community, the complete pipeline — from raw data acquisition through model training and SHAP
explanation — is released as open-source code, allowing the reported results to be verified, extended to
future LTPP releases, and adapted to additional structural or sensor-derived features. The limitations
discussed in @sec-limitations, particularly the sample size available for rigid pavements and reliance on a
single LTPP release, motivate a natural direction for future work: revisiting this framework as additional
LTPP monitoring cycles and Falling Weight Deflectometer coverage accumulate, and incorporating structural
response data beyond load-transfer efficiency, such as full deflection basins, as a further mechanistic input
to explainable IRI prediction.

## Data and Code Availability

The complete reproducible pipeline (data acquisition, cleaning, modeling, and this manuscript) is available on
GitHub at `https://github.com/sabernaseralavi-60/2026_International-Roughness-Index-Prediction-Using-the-LTPP`
under an open license. Raw LTPP data is public domain, distributed by FHWA InfoPave.
